title: Common law
summary: 9084 Contract — unliquidated damages, remoteness, mitigation, and expectation loss.
learningObjectives: Apply Hadley v Baxendale remoteness tests.
Analyse mitigation duty and types of loss.
Calculate expectation, reliance, and restitution measures.

simpleExplanation.title: Putting the innocent party in position
simpleExplanation.summary: Common law damages aim to put the claimant in the position they would have been in if the contract had been performed — subject to remoteness and mitigation.
steps: Remoteness — usual or known special loss.

flashcards.back: **Compensate** innocent party — put in position as if contract performed (expectation loss), not punish breacher.
Loss **arising naturally** from breach in ordinary course of things.
Loss **reasonably contemplated** by both parties at contract time as **probable result** of breach (special circumstances communicated).
Claimant must take **reasonable steps** to minimise loss — cannot recover avoidable loss (*Pilkington v Wood*).
Expectation — profit of bargain; reliance — wasted expenditure; claimant usually chooses higher measure.
Generally **not recoverable** in contract (contrast tort) — exceptions: consumer distress in limited cases (Farley v Skinner).

sections.content: **Common law remedies** (3.4.1) — **Hadley v Baxendale** two limbs for remoteness.

sections.question: A miller sends a broken crankshaft to B for repair, telling B the mill is stopped. B delays return by a week. The mill remains idle, losing £800 profit. A spare shaft was available locally but miller did not buy it. Advise on damages.
sections.solution:
**Case parallel *Hadley v Baxendale*:** Miller's special circumstance (mill stopped) was **communicated** to B.

**Remoteness:** Lost profits from idle mill are **recoverable** under **second limb** — both parties knew delay would stop production.

**Mitigation:** Miller should have bought **locally available spare shaft** — failure to mitigate reduces recoverable loss (*Pilkington v Wood*).

**Calculation:** £800 lost profit **minus** cost of spare shaft miller should have bought (if cheaper than waiting).

**Conclusion:** Miller recovers **avoidable loss only** — not full £800 if spare shaft would have limited downtime at lower cost.

sections.label: Mark a common law remedies question
